text: Intra-abdominal rectosigmoid transection with stump aversion can be an option during rectal surgery in men when rectal transection proves difficult. This video shows the performance of rectal aversion with coloanal anastomosis during a robotic-assisted ultra-low anterior resection. The patient is a forty-nine-year-old male who is diagnosed with rectal cancer by colonoscopy and biopsy. He underwent neoadjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy after complete staging of the lesion.

The surgical team performs a totally robotic ultra-low anterior resection using the Da Vinci S surgical system. The inferior mesenteric artery is ligated, and mesorectal dissection is carried out using a medial to lateral approach. The splenic flexure is mobilized, followed by an anterior resection with total mesorectal excision. The proximal sigmoid colon and mesocolon are transected using endoGI staplers.

After completing the transection, the surgeon confirms the adequate length of the proximal colon. A circular stapler with an anvil is introduced through the anus to the rectal stump. The stapler is opened, and a suture is placed around the gap between the stapler head and anvil head, securing the rectal stump to the circular stapler for withdrawal through the anus.

The surgeon uses traction on the circular stapler to fully evert the rectal stump through the anus. Distal transection is performed at the pectineal line, meeting the abdominal plane of dissection. The total mesorectal excision (TME) specimen is removed, and the left colonic remnant is extracted through the anus and opened.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis using interrupted absorbable sutures, aided by the Lone Star retractor. The colonic position within the abdominal cavity is reviewed laparoscopically. A diverting ileostomy is created at the end of the procedure.
